metadata.summary: Repeatable new-hire onboarding program: preboarding checklist, Day 1 orientation, buddy pairing, 30-60-90 phases with observable artifacts, feedback loops, manager training, and remote adaptations.

content.title: Onboarding Program Design
content.summary: An end-to-end framework for designing and operating a repeatable new-hire onboarding program: preboarding, Day 1 orientation, buddy pairing, 30-60-90 day progression, feedback loops, manager guides, and remote adaptations. Targets organizations hiring at a pace where ad-hoc handoffs produce inconsistent ramp.

why.p: Unstructured onboarding produces variable ramp speed, elevated 90-day attrition, and disconnected new hires. A program-level approach with defined phases, observable goals, and structured feedback loops surfaces problems early (week-1 pulse) rather than at attrition. SHRM data links formal onboarding to 82% higher retention at year one.

list.item: Standing up a repeatable onboarding program for the first time (replacing tribal knowledge).
Hiring velocity of one or more hires per week where ad-hoc approaches diverge.
Rolling out remote-first onboarding where in-office Day-1 rituals do not translate.
After a 90-day-retention drop or new-hire eNPS decline.
M&A integration requiring two onboarding cultures to converge.
One or two hires per year — a checklist suffices; a full program is not worth the maintenance.
C-suite or executive onboarding — bespoke, board-driven; this framework does not apply.
Contractor or agency placements under 90 days — limit to access provisioning and safety.
Crisis backfills replacing a critical departure — collapse into knowledge transfer, not program onboarding.

section.name: program-components
section.p: Eight components of a repeatable new-hire onboarding program with scope and agent-workflow notes for each.

rule.statement: Move all paperwork, equipment shipping, and account provisioning to preboarding; Day 1 must be reserved for human connection, not e-signatures.
rule.rationale: New hires who spend Day 1 on HR paperwork report lower engagement and higher anxiety. Every administrative task completed before Day 1 reduces cognitive load on the highest-anxiety workday.

section.p: Preboarding timeline: send welcome email and next-steps on offer signing; ship equipment 2 weeks before; send schedule and team-intro email 1 week before; send reminder and point-of-contact day before.

rule.statement: Day 1 agenda must prioritize welcome and buddy introduction in the first hour; IT setup and paperwork follow only after the human connection is established.
rule.rationale: First impressions of belonging predict 90-day retention more than tool access. Reversing the order (IT first) signals that the organization values compliance over people.

section.p: Suggested Day 1 flow: 9:00 welcome and buddy intro, 9:30 HR paperwork and benefits, 10:30 IT setup, 12:00 team lunch, 13:30 company overview and culture, 15:00 manager 1:1 on expectations, 16:00 self-paced onboarding.

rule.statement: Assign a buddy who is peer-level and on a different reporting line from the manager; duration 90 days minimum with weekly check-ins in month 1.
rule.rationale: A manager-chosen buddy from the same chain of command replicates the manager relationship rather than providing the independent, informal channel that predicts new-hire integration.

section.p: Buddy scope: culture and unwritten rules, navigating the organization, introducing cross-functional contacts. Not a second manager; the buddy does not evaluate performance.

rule.statement: Each onboarding phase must end with an observable artifact; never accept "understands X" or "is familiar with Y" as a milestone completion criterion.
rule.rationale: Vague verbs like "understand" cannot be verified at the day-30 review; observable artifacts (PR merged, doc written, customer call completed) can.

section.p: Phase structure: days 1-30 learn (context, relationships, tools); days 31-60 contribute (first owned project); days 61-90 execute (independent delivery).

rule.statement: Run a 4-question pulse survey at week 1, detailed survey at day 30, check-in conversation at day 60, and full review including eNPS at day 90.
rule.rationale: Rising attrition shows up in week-1 pulse data first. Waiting for the day-30 survey means losing 3 weeks of correction opportunity.

section.p: Core feedback questions: Did you feel welcomed? Was your role clear? Did you have the tools you needed? How could we improve? Keep week-1 survey to 4 questions maximum.

rule.statement: Train managers on the onboarding timeline and their specific actions at each milestone before the hire's first day; unprepared managers are the leading cause of poor onboarding outcomes.
rule.rationale: Manager preparation (workspace set up, 1:1 schedule, expectations documented) is the variable most correlated with new-hire engagement at day 30 (Gallup).

rule.statement: For remote hires: ship laptop pre-imaged before Day 1, default Day 1 to async-plus-one-synchronous-session, and over-communicate using multiple channels.
rule.rationale: Remote hires who don't receive equipment before Day 1 spend their first week without tools; the resulting frustration is disproportionate to the logistics cost of early shipping.

section.p: Remote-specific risks: isolation (mitigate with virtual coffee and buddy program), culture invisibility (explicit culture sessions and documentation), connection failure (virtual team activities in week 1).

rule.statement: Spread compliance modules (security, harassment, GDPR, SOC 2) across days 5-30; never schedule them all on Day 1.
rule.rationale: Compliance overload on Day 1 crowds out role learning and culture absorption — the two inputs that actually predict ramp speed and retention.

section.reference: Run 5 days before start to verify all 7 preboarding conditions are GREEN before sending the welcome email.

section.name: checklist
section.p: Verifiable action items for each onboarding phase from before Day 1 through month 1, with completion criteria.

rule.statement: All seven preboarding items must be complete before sending the Day-1 schedule to the new hire.
rule.rationale: Sending a Day-1 schedule when equipment has not shipped or accounts are not provisioned sets up a failed first day and damages trust in the organization.

list.item: Equipment ordered and confirmed shipped with tracking number.
Email, Slack, and primary tools accounts created and verified.
Welcome email sent with first-week schedule and point-of-contact name.
Team notified of start date, role, and brief intro bio.
Buddy assigned and briefed on scope and first-week check-in date.
Benefits enrollment instructions sent.
Manager has blocked calendar for Day-1 1:1 and week-1 daily check-ins.
Warm welcome from team within first 30 minutes.
IT setup verified complete (laptop on, tools accessible).
Paperwork and benefit enrollment signed.
Manager 1:1 completed: expectations discussed, first project scope shared.
Introduction to buddy completed.
All recurring key meetings (standups, 1:1s, team syncs) added to calendar.
Access to all required systems verified by the new hire.
Training modules assigned with completion dates.
Week-1 pulse survey sent (4 questions, anonymous).
Introduction to key stakeholders outside immediate team completed.
30-day check-in completed with manager; goals for days 31-60 documented.
Feedback collected and blockers addressed.
Day-30 detailed onboarding survey sent.
Buddy check-in: confirm buddy program is active and useful.

rule.statement: 30-day goals must be documented in a shared artifact before the check-in closes; verbal agreements decay within a week.
rule.rationale: Undocumented goals cannot be referenced at the day-60 review; the hire enters phase 2 without a written contract of what success looks like.

antipattern.description: Documentation-as-onboarding: assigning a new hire a reading list without synchronous context.
antipattern.reason: New hires do not read documentation as a primary learning mode; it is a reference, not an onboarding mechanism. An agent piping docs is a worse experience than a human explanation.

antipattern.description: Buddy program with no check-in cadence or scope definition.
antipattern.reason: Buddy relationships degrade silently when buddies stop checking in; without a scheduled first meeting and defined scope, the buddy becomes a name on a form.

section.p: Limitations and failure modes: 30-60-90 plans default to "learn / contribute / execute" but verbs are too generic to gate progress — require observable artifacts. Buddy programs degrade silently when buddies stop checking in; agents can monitor but cannot repair relationships. Documentation-as-onboarding fails — new hires don't read it; agent piping docs is worse, not better. Cultural transmission resists templating: values, unwritten rules, "how we disagree", "when to escalate" are invisible to checklists. Compliance training overload in week 1 crowds out role learning and culture absorption. Auto-generated 30-60-90 goals miss manager's real expectations and become aspirational fiction.

Remote-first onboarding risks and mitigations: isolation (pair with virtual coffee scheduling and active buddy program), culture invisibility (explicit culture sessions and documentation), connection failures (virtual team activities in week 1, point-of-contact responsiveness SLA). Pre-image and ship laptops before Day 1 for remote hires; default Day 1 to async-plus-one synchronous session. Over-communicate across Slack, email, calendar, and video.

Tools and service integration: BambooHR (SMB default; onboarding tasks first-class), Rippling (best end-to-end provisioning for agents), HiBob (strong workflows; clean API), Sapling (onboarding-specialist; webhook-rich), WorkBright (I-9/paperwork compliance), Lattice/15Five (30-60-90 + check-ins built-in), Donut for Slack (buddy pairing automation), Loom (async welcome videos), Notion/Confluence (de-facto wiki). Okta/Azure AD/JumpCloud for provisioning, Jamf/Kandji for zero-touch laptops. Greenhouse Onboarding bridges ATS→onboarding without re-keying.
section.reference: Automated gate: run 5 days before start; blocks welcome email if any item is RED.

template.purpose: Preboarding readiness checker: validates 7 conditions 5 days before start date; outputs GREEN/RED with blocking items.

checklist.check: Preboarding readiness gate run 5 days before start; all 7 conditions GREEN before Day-1 schedule sent.
Equipment ordered and shipping confirmed with tracking; shipped 2 weeks before start date.
Accounts (email, Slack, tools) created, verified, and configured before Day 1.
Welcome email sent on offer signing; includes first-week schedule and point-of-contact.
Buddy assigned, peer-level, on different reporting line, briefed on 90-day scope with weekly check-in calendar.
Manager calendar blocked for Day-1 1:1 and daily week-1 check-ins before hire's first day.
Day 1 flow prioritizes welcome/buddy introduction first hour; IT setup after human connection.
Week-1 pulse survey (4 questions max) sent by end of week 1; results reviewed by manager + buddy.
Day-30 check-in: goals for days 31-60 documented in shared artifact, not verbal.
Feedback loops running: week-1 pulse, day-30 survey, day-60 check-in, day-90 eNPS review.
Compliance training spread across days 5-30; never all on Day 1.

content.note: Agentic workflow: drive as six-stage pipeline owned by `faion-onboarding-agent`. Stage 1 (preboarding, Sonnet) — kickoff equipment/accounts/welcome. Stage 2 (Day 1 plan, Sonnet) — personalized agenda. Stage 3 (30-60-90 draft, Opus) — combines JD + level + team OKRs into observable goals; manager edits before sending. Stage 4 (cadence reminders, Haiku) — nudges manager/buddy/hire at milestones. Stage 5 (feedback collection, Sonnet) — pulse surveys with coded text analysis. Stage 6 (analytics, Opus) — quarterly cohort review on attrition, ramp metrics. Mandatory human loop on every plan and survey deployment.
